children.value:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import datetime
This subchapter goes over a lot of simple but extremely useful
methods that you will likely use a lot.
To start, let’s load in the same dataset as the first subchapter in the
chapter. As a reminder, this dataset has beer sales across 50 continental states in the US. It is sourced from
Salience and Taxation: Theory and Evidence
by Chetty, Looney, and Kroft (AER 2009), and it includes 7 columns:
: the year the data was recorded
: the quantity of beer consumed, in thousands of gallons
: the ad valorem tax, as a percentage
: the excise tax, represented in dollars per case (24 cans) of beer
: the population of the state, in thousands

text/html.content:

1 AL 1971 37598 69.304600 2.370 3497 4.0
2 AL 1972 42719 67.149190 2.370 3539 4.0
3 AL 1973 46203 63.217026 2.370 3580 4.0
4 AL 1974 49769 56.933796 2.370 3627 4.0
... ... ... ... ... ... ... ...

1704 WY 2000 12595 0.309491 0.045 494 4.0
1705 WY 2001 12808 0.300928 0.045 494 4.0

children.value: To start, we will note that
is designed to work extremely well with
DataFrames and Series. A few examples are shown below.

children.value: helps us easily compute several statistics from a DataFrame.

Finally, there are a whole host of intuitive DataFrame methods that calculate statistics such as the average, standard deviation, median, percentiles, etc. While we cannot possibly describe them all here, we strongly encourage you to google around to learn more about them.
is one link to get you started.

Here are some of the methods used to deal with sorting, counting and uniqueness problems. Again, this list is not meant to be comprehensive, and you are encouraged to google around for other methods you may find useful.

can be used to sort DataFrames and Series! If sorting a DataFrame, you need to specify which column you need to sort by, but there is naturally no such restriction for sorting series. Examples are shown below; note the

children.value: String methods are a great tool for working with string data. They only work on Series. To use them, you add a

after your Series to indicate that you want to use a string method, followed by the method you want to use. Some of the various string methods are given below, but again this list is not comprehensive.
Gives the length of the strings.
Like list slicing, but for strings. Review the

1705    W
1706    W
1707    W

children.value: Splits the strings on the given delimiter. Performs no splits if the delimiter doesn’t exist in the string. Returns a list of all the split substrings. Best understood by looking at an example.

text/plain.content:
68      AZ
102     AR
1574    WA
592     LA
1302    RI
1642    WI
Name: st_name, dtype: object

68      [, Z]
102     [, R]
1574    [W, ]
592     [L, ]
1302     [RI]
1642     [WI]
Name: st_name, dtype: object

children.value: As you can see above, all the strings have been split on the letter
. Strings which don’t have the letter

children.value: objects are incredibly helpful for working with dates. They help us easily perform calculations like add/subtract days/dates, convert timezones, sort dates, etc. We can convert a Series to a DateTime object by using

Notice how we specify the format of our dates to ensure the conversion happens correctly. We don’t always need to do this (especially if we have the year, month and day in a well-known format), but it is good practice to ensure the conversion happens correctly.
column in the DataFrame to a DateTime object and perform some calculations

children.value: Notice how the dates all default to 1st January. Let’s say we know that the measurements were actually done 4 days after the given date for each year. We can implement this in our DataFrame.

children.value: adds to the current day while
replaces the current day. Similar differences exist for
Now, let’s look at how a datapoint is stored.
In the 20th index, the year is 1990. Let’s subtract the dates.

text/plain.content: Timedelta('7305 days 00:00:00')

children.value: Interesting, we can also subtract the year’s directly.
Let’s also try subtracting the months.

The months are subtracted directly. As both of these months were set to June, there is no difference. Instead, to see the number of months between two dates, we must first subtract the years, multiply that by 12, and then subtract the months and add that. The calculation has been done below.
Finally, using DateTime objects also helps us sort dates easily!
